question: What was Apple's iPhone net sales in fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Apple's iPhone net sales were $201.18 billion in fiscal year 2024 (per Note 2 Revenue), essentially flat from $200.58 billion in fiscal 2023.

question: What was Microsoft's total revenue for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Microsoft's total revenue for fiscal year 2024 was $245.12 billion ($245,122 million), consisting of $64.77 billion in product revenue and $180.35 billion in service and other revenue (per the consolidated income statement).

question: What were Tesla's total automotive revenues for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Tesla's total automotive revenues for fiscal year 2024 were $77.07 billion, comprising automotive sales of $72.48 billion, automotive regulatory credits of $2.76 billion, and automotive leasing of $1.83 billion (per Revenue By Source, Item 8).

question: What was NVIDIA's Data Center segment revenue in fiscal year 2026?
reference_answer: NVIDIA's Data Center end-market revenue was $193.74 billion in fiscal year 2026 (per Revenue by End Market table), up 68% from $115.19 billion in fiscal year 2025. Total NVIDIA revenue for FY2026 was $215.9 billion.

question: What were Apple's services net sales in fiscal year 2025?
reference_answer: Apple's services net sales were $109.16 billion in fiscal year 2025 (per Note 2 Revenue), up from $96.17 billion in fiscal year 2024, a 13.5% increase.

question: What was Microsoft's Intelligent Cloud segment revenue in fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Microsoft's Intelligent Cloud segment revenue was $105.36 billion in fiscal year 2024 (per Segment Results of Operations, Item 7), up 20% from $87.91 billion in fiscal 2023, with segment operating income of $49.58 billion. Growth was driven by Azure and other cloud services revenue rising 30%.

question: How many vehicles did Tesla deliver in fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Tesla delivered approximately 1,789,000 consumer vehicles in fiscal year 2024 (per Overview and 2024 Highlights, Item 7), while producing approximately 1,773,000 vehicles. Total revenues for 2024 were $97.69 billion, an increase of $917 million from fiscal 2023.

question: What was Meta's advertising revenue for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Meta's advertising revenue was $160.63 billion in fiscal year 2024 (per Note 2 Revenue, Item 8), up from $131.95 billion in fiscal 2023, a 21.7% increase. Advertising accounted for 98.9% of Family of Apps revenue ($162.36 billion) and 97.6% of Meta's total revenue ($164.50 billion).

question: How much of Apple's 2024 net sales came from deferred revenue at the start of the period?
reference_answer: Apple recognized approximately $7.7 billion of revenue in fiscal year 2024 that was included in deferred revenue as of the start of the period (September 2023), per Note 3 - Earnings Per Share, Item 8. This represents revenue from services and other obligations bundled with prior-period product sales that was earned during fiscal 2024.

question: How does Tesla account for automotive regulatory credits revenue?
reference_answer: Tesla earns tradable automotive regulatory credits under regulations related to zero-emission vehicles (ZEVs), greenhouse gas, fuel economy, and clean fuel, and sells these credits to other regulated entities that need them to comply with emission standards. Tesla recognizes revenue on the sale of regulatory credits at the time control of the credits is transferred to the purchasing party. These credits have negligible incremental costs, and deferred revenue related to their sales was immaterial as of December 31, 2024 and 2023 (per Note 2 - Revenue, Item 8).

question: What is the basis for Microsoft's unearned revenue recognition?
reference_answer: Microsoft's unearned revenue is generally invoiced annually at the beginning of each contract period for multi-year agreements and recognized ratably over the coverage period (per Note 13 - Unearned Revenue, Item 8). It also includes payments received in advance for other offerings, with revenue earned when control of the product or service is transferred to the customer. Unearned revenue comprises mainly volume licensing programs including Software Assurance (SA) and cloud services.

question: What was Meta's stock-based compensation expense for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Meta's stock-based compensation expense was $16.69 billion in fiscal year 2024 (per Consolidated Statements of Cash Flows, Item 8, and MD&A discussion of operating cash flow). Stock-based compensation appears as a non-cash adjustment reconciling Meta's $62.36 billion net income to cash provided by operating activities.

question: Compare NVIDIA's and Microsoft's data center business focus.
reference_answer: NVIDIA frames its data center business as the core of the company, described as a data center scale AI infrastructure company reshaping all industries (Item 1 Business), with end-market Data Center revenue of $193.74 billion in FY2026. Microsoft frames its data center business as the Intelligent Cloud segment ($105.36 billion in FY2024 per Segment Results of Operations), centered on Azure cloud services and server products, with growth driven by Azure consumption-based services (+30% FY2024). NVIDIA's focus is infrastructure (GPUs, networking, full-stack systems); Microsoft's focus is software and cloud services running on that infrastructure.

question: How do Apple and Meta differ in their approach to advertising revenue?
reference_answer: Meta generates substantially all of its revenue from advertising — $160.63 billion of FY2024 total revenue of $164.50 billion (Item 7 / Cost of Revenue and Operating Expenses), displayed on Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and third-party mobile apps. Apple does not break out advertising revenue separately; ad-related revenue (Search Ads, App Store Search, Apple News+, Apple TV+ ads) is bundled into Services net sales ($96.17 billion FY2024). The corpus reflects this asymmetry — Meta has dozens of advertising-focused chunks while Apple's advertising treatment is buried in Services discussion. Apple's influence on Meta is also noted in Meta's risk factors: Apple's privacy/tracking changes (ATT) have adversely affected Meta's ad targeting effectiveness.

question: Compare Tesla's and NVIDIA's R&D spending priorities.
reference_answer: NVIDIA's R&D priorities center on accelerated computing platforms — chip design, GPU architecture, networking (post-Mellanox), and full-stack AI infrastructure systems including Blackwell (FY2026 platform). NVIDIA frames itself as a data center scale AI infrastructure company with R&D spanning hardware (GPUs, DPUs, networking) and software (CUDA, AI Enterprise platform). Tesla's R&D priorities focus on AI software, autonomous driving (FSD/Robotaxi/Cybercab), battery technology, vertically-integrated supply chain, and energy storage products (Powerhub). Both companies position R&D as strategic platform investment, but NVIDIA's R&D yields infrastructure sold to others, while Tesla's R&D feeds vertically-integrated products.

question: How do Microsoft and NVIDIA describe their AI strategy?
reference_answer: NVIDIA's AI strategy centers on extending its technology and platform leadership in AI through a complete end-to-end accelerated computing platform addressing both training and inferencing, sold to all major hyperscalers and AI labs. NVIDIA positions itself as the foundational infrastructure provider — GPUs, networking, software stack (CUDA, AI Enterprise) — enabling generative and agentic AI workloads. Microsoft's AI strategy is application-layer focused: Copilot products across the stack (Microsoft 365 Copilot, Copilot for Security, GitHub Copilot, Windows 365), Azure OpenAI Service as a foundation model offering, and AI infrastructure built on NVIDIA hardware in Azure data centers. Microsoft frames AI as the Future Opportunity driving Microsoft Cloud growth, while NVIDIA frames AI as the workload that demands its accelerated computing platform.

question: Show Apple's net sales by product category for 2024 versus 2023.
reference_answer: Apple's net sales by product category for fiscal 2024 vs fiscal 2023 (per Note 2 Revenue and Products & Services Performance tables): iPhone $201.18B vs $200.58B (flat), Mac $29.98B vs $29.36B (+2.1%), iPad $26.69B vs $28.30B (-5.7%), Wearables/Home/Accessories $37.01B vs $39.85B (-7.1%), Services $96.17B vs $85.20B (+12.9%). Total net sales $391.04B vs $383.29B (+2.0%), driven primarily by Services growth.

question: What was NVIDIA's revenue by geographic region in fiscal year 2026?
reference_answer: NVIDIA's FY2026 revenue by geographic region (customer headquarters location, per Item 1 Business / Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements, Geographic Revenue table): United States $149,617M, Taiwan $42,345M, China (including Hong Kong) $19,677M, Other $4,299M, totaling $215,938M. In Q3 FY2026 NVIDIA changed methodology to base attribution on customer headquarters location rather than bill-to/ship-to.

question: What was Microsoft's revenue by reportable segment for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Microsoft's revenue by reportable segment for fiscal year 2024 (per Segment Results of Operations, Item 7): Productivity and Business Processes $77.73 billion (+12% vs FY2023), Intelligent Cloud $105.36 billion (+20%), and More Personal Computing $62.03 billion (+13%). Total revenue of $245.12 billion (+16%). Productivity and Business Processes growth was driven by Office Commercial and LinkedIn; Intelligent Cloud growth by Azure (+30%); More Personal Computing growth by Activision Blizzard acquisition (closed October 2023).

question: What were Tesla's vehicle deliveries by model for fiscal year 2024?
reference_answer: Tesla delivered approximately 1,789,000 consumer vehicles in fiscal year 2024 and produced approximately 1,773,000 (per Overview and 2024 Highlights, Item 7). The 10-K reports production status by model and region (California: Model S/Model X; Texas: Model Y/Cybertruck; Berlin: Model Y; Shanghai: Model 3/Model Y) but does not break out total delivery counts by specific model within the filing itself; Tesla typically discloses model-level delivery breakdowns in separate quarterly update releases.

question: How does NVIDIA describe what makes its computing platforms hard for competitors to replicate?
reference_answer: NVIDIA describes its computing platforms as hard to replicate through full-stack innovation across the architecture, chip design, system, interconnect, algorithm, and software layers — allowing performance leaps that outpace Moore's Law (per Item 1 Business / Business Strategies). The moat combines pioneered accelerated computing technology, a complete data-center-scale platform (GPUs plus networking plus software), support for more than 4,400 applications across its ecosystem, and the Mellanox acquisition extending into high-performance networking with the data processing unit (DPU). The integration of these layers — hardware, CUDA software, networking, and application ecosystem — is what NVIDIA positions as defensible against competitors.
notes: No exact-term match; tests dense advantage.

question: What does Apple say about risks from concentration in its supplier base?
reference_answer: Apple describes supplier concentration risk through several lenses (per Item 1 Business and Item 1A Risk Factors): a substantial portion of components and products are manufactured by outsourcing partners primarily located outside the United States, with manufacturing concentrated in specific geographies and facilities. Many components are sourced from a single supplier or a small number of suppliers, including custom components developed specifically for new products which may face initial capacity constraints. Apple is also exposed to supplier financial distress, industry consolidation reducing available sources, and disruptions in manufacturing or logistics from natural and man-made disasters, IT failures, commercial disputes, and geopolitical conditions. The 10-K explicitly identifies Concentrations In The Available Sources Of Supply Of Materials And Product as a financial-statement-level disclosure.

question: How does Tesla describe its approach to self-driving technology development?
reference_answer: Tesla describes its self-driving approach as a combined hardware-software-AI development effort (per Item 1 Business / Self-Driving Development And Artificial Intelligence). Tesla maintains extensive in-house R&D capabilities for its Full Self-Driving (FSD) Supervised system, develops most of its in-vehicle software internally with regular over-the-air updates, and operates Cortex — its training cluster at Gigafactory Texas — for FSD neural network training. The 10-K describes Tesla's expectation of evolving toward fully autonomous capability through the purpose-built Robotaxi product (Cybercab), and notes that there are currently no federal U.S. regulations pertaining specifically to self-driving vehicles though NHTSA has published guidelines. Tesla's approach combines real-world data from its vehicle fleet, in-house AI training infrastructure, and continuous over-the-air software deployment.

question: What does Microsoft say about how its AI infrastructure investments are paying off?
reference_answer: Microsoft describes its AI investments as paying off through three primary channels (per Item 7 MD&A and Item 15 Future Opportunity): (1) Revenue growth — fiscal 2025 revenue increased $36.6 billion or 15% over fiscal 2024, with Intelligent Cloud revenue growth driven by Azure which incorporates AI capabilities; (2) Product expansion — Copilot for Security, Microsoft 365 Copilot, Windows 365, and GitHub Copilot generate AI-attributable revenue and customer adoption; (3) Strategic positioning — Microsoft is a major investor in OpenAI with reciprocal revenue-sharing arrangements and rights to OpenAI intellectual property including models and infrastructure for integration into its products. Microsoft frames AI as the platform wave driving Microsoft Cloud growth across its solution areas.

question: How did Apple's services revenue grow from 2023 to 2024, and what factors drove the growth?
reference_answer: Apple's services net sales grew from $85.20 billion in fiscal 2023 to $96.17 billion in fiscal 2024 — an increase of $10.97 billion or 12.9% (per Note 2 Revenue, Item 8, and Products & Services Performance, Item 7). Services growth was the primary driver of Apple's overall revenue increase in fiscal 2024: net sales in the Americas, Europe, and Greater China each increased compared to fiscal 2023 due primarily to higher net sales of Services (and in Europe's case, also iPhone). The 10-K does not break out which specific services drove the growth at the line-item level — Apple's Services category aggregates App Store, iCloud, advertising, AppleCare, Apple Music, Apple TV+, Apple Arcade, payment services, and other offerings.
notes: Needs segment table AND MD&A prose.

question: What were Tesla's main operational challenges in 2024 and how did they affect financial results?
reference_answer: Tesla's main operational challenges in fiscal year 2024 included production ramp issues with new vehicles (notably Cybertruck), supply chain pressures, raw material inflation, labor and shipping disruptions, and increased competition in the EV market (per Item 1A Risk Factors and Item 7 MD&A). These factors contributed to a modest financial outcome: total revenues grew only $917 million in 2024 (approximately 1%) to $97.69 billion, with automotive sales revenue declining year-over-year (from $78.5 billion to $72.5 billion) due to pricing actions taken to maintain delivery volumes. The decline in automotive revenue was offset by strong growth in energy generation and storage (deployments rose to 31.4 GWh) and in services and other revenue, demonstrating Tesla's increasing diversification beyond pure automotive sales.
